title: Marble loaf
subtitle: Vanilla and chocolate batters swirled in a loaf tin
excerpt: Marble loaf uses a single base batter split into two — half stays vanilla, half gets cocoa powder. The two are spooned alternately into the tin then swirled once with a skewer to create the marble pattern. Over-swirling loses the contrast.
sourceNotes: Marble cake has appeared in German (Marmorkuchen) and British domestic baking since at least the mid-nineteenth century. Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136 — striped and marbled cakes appear in the cakes chapter as a variant on enriched pound cake.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze sliced and wrapped. Defrost at room temperature.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Bake the day before. Store in an airtight tin — it slices more cleanly the next day.

glossaryTerms.definition: The technique of creating a swirled two-tone pattern inside a cake by alternately spooning two differently flavoured batters into the tin, then drawing a skewer or knife through the surface in S-curves once or twice. The key is restraint — one or two passes with the skewer creates the classic marbled look; more than that blends the batters into a single muddy colour.

content.text: is done with a single pass of a skewer after both batters are in the tin. Resist the urge to swirl more than twice — the contrast between vanilla and chocolate disappears quickly and you end up with a beige loaf rather than a marble one.

Heat the oven to 180°C (160°C fan). Line a 900 g (2 lb) loaf tin with baking paper. Beat {{unsalted-butter}} and {{caster-sugar}} until pale and fluffy. Add {{vanilla-extract}}, then beat in the {{eggs}} one at a time. Fold in {{self-raising-flour}} and {{whole-milk}}. Spoon half the batter into a separate bowl. Stir {{cocoa-powder}} into the second bowl and mix until uniform.

Drop alternate spoonfuls of vanilla and chocolate batter into the tin — 3 or 4 spoons of each, alternating, so the tin fills evenly. Draw a skewer or knife through the batter in two or three S-shapes lengthwise. Do not stir or fold. Bake for 45–50 minutes until a skewer inserted in the centre comes out clean. Cool in the tin for 10 minutes, then turn onto a rack.
